title: Isaiah Chapter 23
subtitle: The Oracle Against Tyre
structureSubtitle: See how commercial pride meets God's purpose

verses.text: A prophecy against Tyre: Wail, you ships of Tarshish! For Tyre is destroyed and left without house or harbor. From the land of Cyprus word has come to them.
Be silent, you people of the island and you merchants of Sidon, whom the seafarers have enriched.
On the great waters came the grain of the Shihor; the harvest of the Nile was the revenue of Tyre, and she became the marketplace of the nations.
Be ashamed, Sidon, and you fortress of the sea, for the sea has spoken: 'I have neither been in labor nor given birth; I have neither reared sons nor brought up daughters.'
When the report reaches Egypt, they will be in anguish at the report from Tyre.
Cross over to Tarshish; wail, you people of the island.
Is this your city of revelry, the old, old city, whose feet have taken her to settle in far-off lands?
Who planned this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns, whose merchants are princes, whose traders are renowned in the earth?
The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down her pride in all her splendor and to humble all who are renowned on the earth.
Till your land as they do along the Nile, Daughter Tarshish, for you no longer have a harbor.
The LORD has stretched out his hand over the sea and made its kingdoms tremble. He has given an order concerning Phoenicia that her fortresses be destroyed.
He said, 'No more of your reveling, Virgin Daughter Sidon, now crushed! Up, cross over to Cyprus; even there you will find no rest.'

Look at the land of the Babylonians, this people that is now of no account! The Assyrians have made it a place for desert creatures; they raised up their siege towers, they stripped its fortresses bare and turned it into a ruin.
Wail, you ships of Tarshish; your fortress is destroyed!
At that time Tyre will be forgotten for seventy years, the span of a king's life. But at the end of these seventy years, it will happen to Tyre as in the song of the prostitute:
'Take up a harp, walk through the city, you forgotten prostitute; play the harp well, sing many a song, so you will be remembered.'
At the end of seventy years, the LORD will deal with Tyre. She will return to her lucrative prostitution and will ply her trade with all the kingdoms on the face of the earth.
Yet her profit and her earnings will be set apart for the LORD; they will not be stored up or hoarded. Her profits will go to those who live before the LORD, for abundant food and fine clothes.

1.seeing: The oracle opens with a dramatic command: ships returning from Tarshish (likely Spain) discover their home port is gone. News arrives from Cyprus—the first stop on the route home. The destruction is total: no house, no harbor. Tyre's famous port, the hub of Mediterranean trade, has been wiped out.
1.life: Have you ever returned to find something you relied on completely gone? The ships of Tarshish represent all who build security on economic systems. When harbors of worldly security vanish, where do you turn?
1.teach: God addresses commercial empires at the point of their greatest confidence—their ships and harbors. No economic system is too big to fail when God decrees its end. Wailing replaces profit margins.

2.seeing: Sidon, Tyre's sister city and trade partner, is told to 'be silent'—stunned into speechlessness. The merchants who grew wealthy through seafaring trade now have nothing to say. The silence contrasts with the busy commerce that once filled their ports.
2.life: When life strips away everything you've built, silence can be the first honest response. Have you experienced a loss so profound that words failed? Sometimes silence before God is the beginning of wisdom.
2.teach: The merchants of Sidon were enriched by seafaring trade, but enrichment without God is fragile. Silence here isn't worship—it's shock. The difference between holy silence and stunned silence is the posture of the heart.

3.seeing: Tyre's wealth came from controlling trade routes—Egyptian grain from the Nile delta flowed through her ports. She became the 'marketplace of the nations,' a title of immense economic power. Her position was the ancient equivalent of a global trading hub.
3.life: Being a 'marketplace of nations' sounds impressive until God redefines success. What marketplace have you built your identity around—career, status, financial networks? These can be gifts, but they make poor foundations.
3.teach: Tyre's role as commercial middleman made her wealthy but also vulnerable. When you become the marketplace rather than a participant in God's economy, you confuse your role with your identity. Revenue is not the same as significance.

4.seeing: The sea itself personified speaks: it has produced nothing lasting. Sidon's 'fortress of the sea' trusted in maritime power, but the sea disowns her. The imagery of barrenness—no labor, no birth, no children—portrays total fruitlessness despite apparent productivity.
4.life: Busy productivity can mask deep barrenness. Are you producing much but bearing no lasting fruit? The sea's lament challenges us: all our commercial activity may leave nothing of eternal value behind.
4.teach: The personified sea's denial of offspring is devastating. Sidon thought the sea was her mother, nourishing her commerce. But the sea belongs to God, not to merchants. Even natural resources serve God's purposes, not human ambition.

5.seeing: Egypt, itself a superpower, trembles at Tyre's fall. When a major trading partner collapses, the shockwaves ripple across the ancient world. Egypt's anguish reveals how interconnected the ancient economy was—Tyre's destruction affects everyone.
5.life: Economic collapses create fear far beyond their epicenter. When institutions you depend on shake, do you find your peace in market stability or in the God who governs markets? Others' crises often expose our own vulnerabilities.
5.teach: Egypt's anguish shows that worldly powers mourn each other's losses because their fates are intertwined. But God's people are called to a different kind of interdependence—one rooted in His kingdom, not commercial alliances.

6.seeing: The refugees are told to flee to Tarshish—the farthest western port known. But even fleeing, they must wail. There's no escaping the grief. The 'people of the island' (coastland dwellers) who once celebrated now become displaced wanderers.
6.life: Running from grief never outpaces it. Have you tried to escape pain by changing locations or circumstances? This verse gently warns that geographical solutions don't address spiritual problems.
6.teach: The instruction to 'cross over to Tarshish' while wailing shows judgment follows the proud wherever they go. Distance from the site of ruin doesn't equal distance from God's purposes. You cannot sail away from divine sovereignty.

7.seeing: The rhetorical question drips with irony. Tyre was ancient even in Isaiah's day—'the old, old city' whose colonies dotted the Mediterranean. Her 'feet' carried her influence to far-off settlements. Now this city of revelry faces silence.
7.life: Long history and wide influence don't guarantee permanence. Is there something in your life you assume will always be there because it's 'always been there'? Age and reach don't equal invincibility.
7.teach: Tyre's antiquity and colonial reach made her residents assume permanence. But 'city of revelry' reveals the problem: celebration without gratitude to God becomes self-indulgence. Ancient roots mean nothing when the soil is pride.

8.seeing: This pivotal question asks: who could bring down Tyre, 'the bestower of crowns'? Her merchants were princes; her traders were earth's celebrities. The question builds suspense—surely no earthly power could topple this commercial titan.
8.life: Who controls the outcomes in your life? When you feel untouchable—successful, connected, influential—this question pierces the illusion. The 'bestower of crowns' is herself subject to a higher King.
8.teach: Tyre didn't just trade goods; she 'bestowed crowns'—she made and unmade kings through economic power. Yet the question 'Who planned this?' implies someone greater than commerce orchestrates history. Economic kingmakers answer to the King of kings.

9.seeing: The answer thunders: 'The LORD Almighty planned it.' Not chance, not rival nations—God Himself targeted Tyre's pride. His purpose: to humble 'all who are renowned on the earth.' This is the theological center of the oracle, revealing God's sovereignty over global commerce.
9.life: Every empire of pride has an expiration date set by God. Where does pride in your achievements need humbling? The LORD Almighty doesn't just observe commercial empires—He actively plans their correction when splendor becomes self-worship.
9.teach: This verse is the hinge of the entire oracle. The question of verse 8 finds its answer: God sovereignly governs commercial empires. 'Planned' (Hebrew: ya'ats) shows deliberate divine counsel, not reactive punishment. God's purpose in humbling pride is ultimately redemptive.

10.seeing: Daughter Tarshish is told to return to agriculture—'till your land as they do along the Nile.' Without Tyre's harbor, the western colonies lose their commercial lifeline. Maritime traders must become farmers. The reversal is total.
10.life: When your professional identity is stripped away, what remains? Being told to 'till the land' after running a shipping empire is humbling. Yet honest labor close to the earth can be more grounding than global commerce.
10.teach: The shift from maritime trade to agricultural labor represents God's ability to completely reorder economies. Tarshish's colonies depended on Tyre's harbor; without it, they must find new ways to survive. God dismantles systems of pride but provides alternative paths.

11.seeing: God's hand stretches over the sea—the very element Tyre trusted. He makes kingdoms tremble and orders Phoenicia's fortresses destroyed. The image of God's hand over the sea echoes the Exodus, where He parted waters and overthrew Egypt's army.
11.life: The hand that parts seas also shakes kingdoms. Is God's sovereign hand a comfort or a challenge to you right now? His authority over seas and nations means nothing is beyond His reach—including your situation.
11.teach: God 'stretches out his hand over the sea'—a phrase loaded with Exodus imagery. The same God who delivered Israel through the Red Sea now judges maritime empires through the same waters. The sea obeys its Maker, not its merchants.

12.seeing: Sidon is called 'Virgin Daughter'—once untouched, now crushed. The command to flee to Cyprus (Kittim) comes with a devastating addition: 'even there you will find no rest.' There is no safe harbor from God's judgment.
12.life: The promise of 'no rest' apart from God echoes through Scripture. Are you seeking rest in escapes and distractions? True rest isn't found in better circumstances but in surrendering to God's purposes.
12.teach: The phrase 'even there you will find no rest' is theologically significant. Rest in Scripture is tied to God's presence and obedience (Psalm 95, Hebrews 4). Those fleeing God's judgment cannot find rest because rest itself is God's gift, not a geographical location.

13.seeing: A parenthetical glance at Babylon: the Assyrians have already reduced it to ruins, a haunt for desert creatures. This serves as a warning—if mighty Babylon can fall, how much more Tyre? The Chaldeans (Babylonians) themselves become God's instrument of judgment.
13.life: Today's superpower is tomorrow's ruin. This verse is a sobering reminder that no nation, no institution, no career is permanent. What feels invincible now may become 'a place for desert creatures' in God's timing.
13.teach: The reference to Babylon's own desolation by Assyria serves a dual purpose: it shows that God uses nations as instruments against each other, and it warns Tyre that even the agents of judgment are themselves subject to judgment.

14.seeing: The oracle circles back to its opening: 'Wail, you ships of Tarshish!' This bookend (inclusio) frames the entire judgment. The fortress—Tyre itself, the stronghold of maritime commerce—is destroyed. The repetition emphasizes finality.
14.life: When God repeats a message, pay attention. The bookend structure of 'Wail, ships of Tarshish' at both the beginning and here drives home the certainty of judgment. Is there a repeated warning in your life you've been ignoring?
14.teach: The literary inclusio (matching v1 and v14) signals the completion of the judgment section. Everything between these bookends details why and how Tyre falls. The repetition isn't redundancy—it's emphasis. God's word accomplishes what He sends it to do.

15.seeing: A surprising turn: Tyre's judgment has a time limit—seventy years, 'the span of a king's life.' This echoes Judah's seventy-year exile in Babylon (Jeremiah 25:11). After judgment comes a strange restoration described through the metaphor of a forgotten prostitute's song.
15.life: God sets time limits on judgment. Seventy years is long but not forever. Are you in a season that feels like exile? This verse whispers that God has an 'end of these years' planned. Patience in discipline leads to restoration.
15.teach: The seventy-year period connects Tyre's fate to Judah's Babylonian exile, suggesting God works on consistent timelines. The prostitute metaphor is jarring but honest—Tyre's commerce was a kind of spiritual prostitution, selling herself to every nation.

16.seeing: The prostitute's song is darkly humorous: 'Take up a harp, walk through the city... so you will be remembered.' Tyre, once the world's most famous trading city, must now sing for attention like a forgotten entertainer. Her former glory has become desperate self-promotion.
16.life: The desperate need to 'be remembered' haunts every ego. When your influence fades, what remains? This verse exposes the emptiness of fame built on commerce alone. True significance comes from being known by God, not remembered by markets.
16.teach: The prostitute song metaphor is biting social commentary. Tyre's trade relationships were transactional, not covenantal. Like a prostitute who must constantly seek new clients, Tyre's commerce lacked the permanence of faithful relationship with God.

17.seeing: After seventy years, the LORD Himself 'deals with' Tyre—the verb suggests divine attention, not abandonment. Tyre returns to trade ('lucrative prostitution'), plying commerce with all nations. But this return is under God's watchful eye, not Tyre's autonomous ambition.
17.life: God can restore what He has judged—but restoration looks different than the original. Tyre trades again, but now under God's sovereign direction. Has God restored something in your life? Notice how the restored version serves His purposes, not just yours.
17.teach: The LORD 'deals with' Tyre—a phrase suggesting purposeful engagement. God doesn't forget judged nations; He revisits them. Tyre's commercial restoration isn't permission to return to prideful autonomy but preparation for the surprising redemption of verse 18.

18.seeing: The stunning conclusion: Tyre's profits become 'set apart for the LORD.' Not stored, not hoarded, but directed to 'those who live before the LORD' for food and clothing. Commerce itself is redeemed—trade profits fund God's people. This is the oracle's redemptive twist.
18.life: What if your earnings were 'set apart for the LORD'? This verse reimagines commerce as worship. Your work, your profits, your trade can serve God's people. The question isn't whether you earn but how your earnings are directed.
18.teach: This is the theological climax: commercial wealth, once the vehicle of pride, becomes holy to the LORD. Isaiah envisions a future where even pagan trade serves God's kingdom. Profits 'not hoarded' but given to sustain God's servants—this is the redemption of economics itself.

from: Ezekiel 27:25-36 - The ships of Tarshish serve as carriers for your wares; all who trade on the seas wail over Tyre
Psalm 48:7 - You destroyed them like ships of Tarshish shattered by an east wind
to: Revelation 18:17-19 - Every sea captain and sailor cried out when they saw the smoke of Babylon's burning
James 4:13-14 - You who say 'Today or tomorrow we will go to this city and carry on business'—you do not know what tomorrow will bring
1.context: The wailing ships of Tarshish prefigure Revelation's merchants weeping over Babylon's fall. Both Tyre and Revelation's Babylon represent commercial empires that seemed invincible until God decreed their end.

from: Ezekiel 28:21-22 - Son of man, set your face against Sidon and prophesy against her
Joel 3:4-6 - What have you against me, Tyre and Sidon? You sold the people of Judah to the Greeks
to: Matthew 11:21-22 - Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the miracles done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented
Acts 12:20 - Herod had been quarreling with the people of Tyre and Sidon, who depended on the king's country for food
2.context: Sidon appears throughout Scripture as a city under judgment, yet Jesus implies Tyre and Sidon would have been more responsive to miracles than Israel's own cities—a stunning reversal of expectations.

from: Ezekiel 27:3-4 - Tyre, you said 'I am perfect in beauty'; your domain was on the high seas
Genesis 41:56-57 - Joseph opened all the storehouses and sold grain to the Egyptians; all the world came to Egypt to buy grain
to: Revelation 18:3 - The merchants of the earth grew rich from her excessive luxuries
1 Timothy 6:9-10 - Those who want to get rich fall into temptation and a trap; the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil
3.context: Tyre as 'marketplace of the nations' anticipates Revelation's Babylon whose merchants grew rich from her luxuries. Both depict how controlling trade creates dangerous illusions of self-sufficiency.

from: Psalm 107:23-27 - Those who went out on the sea in ships saw the works of the LORD; He stilled the storm to a whisper
Isaiah 54:1 - Sing, barren woman, you who never bore a child; burst into song, shout for joy
to: Matthew 7:24-27 - Everyone who hears these words of mine and does not put them into practice is like a foolish man who built his house on sand
Galatians 4:27 - Be glad, barren woman who bears no children; break forth and cry aloud, you who are not in labor
4.context: The sea's denial of fruitfulness for Sidon contrasts with Isaiah 54's barren woman who will rejoice. Fruitlessness from pride leads to shame; fruitlessness surrendered to God leads to unexpected abundance.

from: Ezekiel 30:9 - Messengers will go out from me in ships to frighten Cush out of her complacency; anguish will take hold of them
Isaiah 19:1-4 - A prophecy against Egypt: the LORD rides on a swift cloud and is coming to Egypt; the idols of Egypt tremble
to: Revelation 18:9-10 - The kings of the earth who committed adultery with her will weep and mourn when they see the smoke of her burning
Luke 21:25-26 - Nations will be in anguish and perplexity at the roaring and tossing of the sea; people will faint from terror
5.context: Egypt's anguish at Tyre's report foreshadows how the fall of great commercial centers sends tremors through interconnected nations—a pattern repeated in Revelation's vision of end-times Babylon.

from: Amos 9:1-4 - Not one of them will escape; though they climb up to the heavens, I will bring them down
Jonah 1:3 - Jonah ran away from the LORD and headed for Tarshish
to: Hebrews 2:3 - How shall we escape if we ignore so great a salvation?
Revelation 6:15-16 - They called to the mountains and the rocks: Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who sits on the throne
6.context: Fleeing to Tarshish—the farthest known destination—echoes Jonah's futile flight from God. Neither prophet nor proud city can outrun divine purpose. There is no port beyond God's reach.

from: Ezekiel 26:17 - How you are destroyed, city of renown, peopled by men of the sea!
Lamentations 2:15 - Is this the city that was called the perfection of beauty, the joy of the whole earth?
to: Revelation 18:16-17 - Woe! Woe to you, great city, dressed in fine linen, purple and scarlet! In one hour such great wealth has been brought to ruin!
1 John 2:16-17 - The world and its desires pass away, but whoever does the will of God lives forever
7.context: The mournful question 'Is this your city of revelry?' echoes Lamentations over Jerusalem and anticipates Revelation's lament over Babylon. Ancient cities of joy become object lessons in the transience of worldly glory.

from: Ezekiel 28:2-5 - In the pride of your heart you say 'I am a god'; by your wisdom and understanding you have gained wealth
Daniel 4:30 - Is not this the great Babylon I have built as the royal residence, by my mighty power and for the glory of my majesty?
to: Acts 17:26-27 - From one man God made all the nations, determining the times set for them and the exact places where they should live
Romans 9:20-21 - Who are you, a human being, to talk back to God? Does not the potter have the right to make from the same lump of clay?
8.context: The question 'Who planned this?' challenges every commercial empire's assumption of autonomy. Both Tyre and Nebuchadnezzar's Babylon claimed self-made glory, only to discover a divine Planner behind all history.

from: Isaiah 14:24-27 - The LORD Almighty has sworn: Surely as I have planned, so it will be; as I have purposed, so it will happen
Proverbs 16:18 - Pride goes before destruction, a haughty spirit before a fall
to: Philippians 2:9-11 - God exalted him to the highest place, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow
1 Peter 5:5-6 - God opposes the proud but shows grace to the humble; humble yourselves under God's mighty hand
9.context: The LORD Almighty's deliberate plan to humble pride finds its fullest expression in the gospel: God exalts the humble Christ and calls all the proud to bow. Divine planning governs both judgment and redemption.

from: Genesis 3:17-19 - Cursed is the ground because of you; through painful toil you will eat food from it
Isaiah 2:12-17 - The LORD Almighty has a day in store for all the proud and lofty; they will be humbled
to: 2 Thessalonians 3:10-12 - If anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat; settle down and earn the food you eat
Matthew 23:12 - Those who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted
10.context: The command to return to agricultural labor echoes Eden's curse—from garden commerce to ground-level toil. Yet honest labor is also God's provision. Humbling isn't destruction; it can be redirection toward simpler faithfulness.

from: Exodus 15:6-8 - Your right hand, LORD, was majestic in power; by the blast of your nostrils the waters piled up
Psalm 46:6 - Nations are in uproar, kingdoms fall; He lifts His voice, the earth melts
to: Colossians 1:16-17 - In him all things were created; in him all things hold together
Revelation 11:15 - The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah
11.context: God stretching His hand over the sea recalls the Exodus. The same sovereign hand that delivered Israel now judges maritime empires. All kingdoms—land or sea—tremble before the LORD whose authority knows no boundary.

from: Deuteronomy 28:65 - Among those nations you will find no repose, no resting place for the sole of your foot
Lamentations 1:3 - Judah has gone into exile; among the nations she finds no resting place
to: Hebrews 4:9-11 - There remains a Sabbath-rest for the people of God; let us make every effort to enter that rest
Matthew 11:28-30 - Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest
12.context: Finding 'no rest' even in Cyprus echoes the restlessness of exile and anticipates Jesus' invitation to true rest. Human restlessness apart from God drives the biblical narrative toward Christ, who alone gives soul-rest.

from: Isaiah 13:19-22 - Babylon, the jewel of kingdoms, will be overthrown by God like Sodom and Gomorrah; desert creatures will lie there
Habakkuk 1:5-6 - I am raising up the Babylonians, that ruthless and impetuous people
to: Revelation 17:16-17 - God has put it into their hearts to accomplish his purpose by agreeing to give the beast their power to rule
Romans 13:1 - There is no authority except that which God has established
13.context: God uses Babylon as an instrument against Tyre, yet Babylon itself faces judgment. This pattern—God using flawed nations as instruments while holding them accountable—runs from Isaiah through Revelation.

from: Ezekiel 26:3-5 - I am against you, Tyre, and I will bring many nations against you like the sea casting up its waves
Amos 1:9-10 - For three sins of Tyre, even for four, I will not relent; I will send fire on the walls of Tyre
to: Revelation 18:21-23 - A mighty angel picked up a boulder and threw it into the sea: With such violence the great city of Babylon will be thrown down, never to be found again
2 Peter 3:10-11 - The day of the Lord will come like a thief; the heavens will disappear with a roar; since everything will be destroyed, what kind of people ought you to be?
14.context: The repeated cry 'Wail, ships of Tarshish' bookends the judgment and anticipates Revelation's final destruction of commercial Babylon. Both passages challenge: if earthly fortresses are destroyed, what should we build our lives on?

from: Jeremiah 25:11-12 - This whole country will become a desolate wasteland, and these nations will serve the king of Babylon seventy years
2 Chronicles 36:21 - The land enjoyed its sabbath rests; all the time of its desolation it rested, until the seventy years were completed
to: Luke 4:18-19 - He has sent me to proclaim freedom for the prisoners and the year of the Lord's favor
2 Corinthians 6:2 - I tell you, now is the time of God's favor, now is the day of salvation
15.context: The seventy-year period links Tyre's fate to Judah's exile and ultimately to Christ's proclamation of God's favorable year. God's judgments have expiration dates; His mercy outlasts His wrath.

from: Proverbs 7:10-12 - Then out came a woman to meet him, dressed like a prostitute and with crafty intent; she is loud and defiant
Hosea 2:5 - Their mother has been unfaithful and has conceived them in disgrace; she said, I will go after my lovers who give me food and water
to: Revelation 17:1-2 - Come, I will show you the punishment of the great prostitute who sits by many waters; the kings of the earth committed adultery with her
2 Corinthians 5:17 - If anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come; the old has gone, the new is here
16.context: The prostitute metaphor for commercial Tyre connects to Hosea's unfaithful Israel and Revelation's great prostitute Babylon. But the trajectory moves toward redemption—verse 18 shows profits becoming holy, and in Christ, the old passes away.

from: Ezekiel 29:13-14 - At the end of forty years I will gather the Egyptians from the nations where they were scattered; I will restore the fortunes of Egypt
Jeremiah 29:10 - When seventy years are completed for Babylon, I will come to you and fulfill my good promise to bring you back
to: Romans 11:25-26 - Israel has experienced a hardening in part until the full number of the Gentiles has come in; and in this way all Israel will be saved
Acts 3:21 - Heaven must receive him until the time comes for God to restore everything, as He promised long ago
17.context: God's dealing with Tyre after seventy years parallels His restoration of Judah and foreshadows the ultimate restoration promised in Acts. Even pagan nations experience God's restorative attention after seasons of judgment.

from: Psalm 72:10-11 - May the kings of Tarshish and of distant shores bring tribute to Him; may all kings bow down to Him
Isaiah 60:5-7 - The wealth on the seas will be brought to you; to you the riches of the nations will come
to: Revelation 21:24-26 - The nations will walk by its light, and the kings of the earth will bring their splendor into it; the glory and honor of the nations will be brought into it
Matthew 6:19-21 - Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth; store up for yourselves treasures in heaven; for where your treasure is, there your heart will be also
18.context: Tyre's profits becoming holy to the LORD is one of Scripture's most remarkable redemptive visions. It anticipates Revelation 21 where nations bring their glory into the New Jerusalem. Commerce itself is redeemed—not abolished but redirected toward God's purposes.

theme: God's sovereign judgment humbles the proud merchant city of Tyre, yet ultimately redeems her commerce—profits once hoarded become holy to the LORD

1.name: Wail, Ships of Tarshish!
1.transition: Tyre destroyed, Sidon's harvest gone, even Egypt mourns—the great commercial hub is silenced

2.name: The Once-Joyful City
2.transition: Who planned this against Tyre the bestower of crowns? The LORD Almighty, to humble all pride

3.name: No Refuge Anywhere
3.transition: Even Kittim (Cyprus) offers no rest—God's hand stretches over every sea and shore

4.name: The Chaldeans' Role
4.transition: Babylon serves as God's instrument against Tyre—even tools of judgment face their own reckoning

5.name: Tyre's Restoration After Seventy Years
5.transition: Tyre returns to trade, but her profits become holy to the LORD—commerce redeemed for God's people

hinge.explanation: Theological Center (v9) — The LORD Almighty planned it: God's sovereignty over commercial empires and the humbling of all worldly pride

turn.explanation: Redemptive Turn (v18) — Tyre's profits become holy to the LORD: commerce itself is redeemed, not destroyed, but redirected to serve God's people

structureModal.title: Chapter 23 Structure
structureModal.subtitle: The Oracle Against Tyre
structureModal.intro: Isaiah 23 follows a concentric pattern that moves from the shock of Tyre's commercial collapse inward to the theological center — 'The LORD Almighty planned it' — then back out through the consequences of judgment, before ending with a stunning redemptive turn. The chapter opens and closes with the ships of Tarshish, creating a literary frame (inclusio) around the entire oracle. Tyre was the ancient world's greatest trading hub, and this oracle asks the question every commercial empire must face: who really controls the marketplace?

sections.label: A (v1-5)
sections.name: Wailing Over Tyre's Fall
sections.description: Ships returning from Tarshish discover their home port is gone — no house, no harbor. Sidon is stunned silent, Egypt trembles, and the sea itself disowns its children

sections.label: B (v6-9)
sections.name: Who Planned This?
sections.description: Refugees flee to Tarshish while a piercing question builds: who could topple the city that crowned kings and whose merchants were princes? The answer thunders back — the LORD Almighty planned it, to humble all pride

sections.label: CENTER (v9)
sections.name: The LORD Almighty Planned It
sections.description: The theological heart of the oracle — behind Tyre's fall stands not chance or rival nations but the deliberate plan of God to bring down pride in all its splendor
sections.centerQuote: The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down her pride in all her splendor and to humble all who are renowned on the earth.

sections.label: B' (v10-12)
sections.name: No Harbor, No Rest
sections.description: Maritime traders must become farmers, God's hand stretches over the sea making kingdoms tremble, and even fleeing to Cyprus brings no rest — there is no safe harbor from divine judgment

sections.label: A' (v13-14)
sections.name: Wailing Revisited
sections.description: Babylon's own ruin serves as a warning, and the oracle circles back to its opening cry — 'Wail, you ships of Tarshish; your fortress is destroyed!' — sealing the judgment with a literary bookend

sections.label: D (v15-18)
sections.name: Seventy Years and Redemption
sections.description: After seventy years of silence, Tyre returns to trade — but now her profits are set apart for the LORD, not hoarded. Commerce itself is redeemed as trade earnings feed and clothe those who serve God

parallels.explanation: Both sections open with 'Wail, you ships of Tarshish!' creating the oracle's literary frame. In A, the ships discover Tyre is gone; in A', the wailing is confirmed and finalized. Between these bookends, the entire case against commercial pride is laid out. The repetition drives home the certainty: what God plans, God accomplishes.

Section B asks the pivotal question — who could bring down the city that bestowed crowns? — and answers it with God's sovereign plan. Section B' shows the consequences of that plan: harbors gone, fortresses destroyed, and no rest anywhere. The question of B receives its lived-out answer in B'. God doesn't just plan judgment; He executes it thoroughly.

Verse 9 is the theological hinge on which everything turns. Before it, Tyre looks invincible — ancient, wealthy, world-spanning. After it, Tyre is helpless — harborless, restless, reduced to farming. The single sentence 'The LORD Almighty planned it' transforms an economic collapse into a theological statement: no commercial empire operates outside God's sovereign counsel.

The final section breaks the pattern of judgment with an unexpected twist: Tyre's commerce is restored but redirected. Her profits become 'set apart for the LORD' — not stored or hoarded but given to sustain God's servants. This is the redemption of economics itself: wealth that once fueled pride now feeds the faithful. The oracle that began with wailing ends with provision.

structureModal.closing: Isaiah 23 traces the arc from commercial pride through divine judgment to surprising redemption. Tyre's story warns every marketplace and economy: the LORD Almighty plans the rise and fall of commercial empires, and no amount of wealth makes a nation independent of God. Yet the final verse offers hope that even the profits of pride can be redirected toward holy purposes — that commerce itself can be redeemed when its earnings serve the LORD rather than human ambition.
